THE SMELL — RON SEIDEL
0:00 / 0:00

The Smell

Words, photographs & narration by Ron Seidel

It’s the smell.

I still feel it.

Now it happens at a Dick’s Sporting Goods on Route 22 in Union. Years ago it was a Sports Authority in Paramus. Before that, Birkenmeier’s in Hackensack. Even earlier, Mega Sports in Haifa.

The smell of opportunity.

Of — well — false hope.

That these are what I need. These’ll make me better. These’ll change my game.

THESE… are what’s keeping me from being the best.

It’s false hope but it’s hope nonetheless.

I remember the want.

The not getting everything I asked for. The buildup.

And then BOOM. My parents buying me something. Opening the box. Taking the tissue paper out. Cutting the tags.

It’s the choice.

Which brand? Which style? What color?

Their logos.

The swoosh signaled I thought I’d made it.

Check. Done.

Three lines meant I’m on my way. I’m not the empire. I’m making myself.

It’s about design. The smaller companies.

The Umbro symmetry. The instant recognition of Diadora and its similarity to Puma’s mark.

Kappa’s couple sitting back-to-back in black and white.

It’s noticing the silhouette of breasts.

How your mind is shaped.

Sony is the best. Sharp is also good. Volvo is safe, Toyota reliable, Apple just works.

Again, it’s color: Man U red is good. Barça blue and maroonish red is good. Chelsea and Real Madrid blues aren’t. Ajax red with white sleeves: very good.

My early years faves: Maccabi Haifa green standing for peace and progress. And the unquestionable supremacy of Brazil yellow with a splash of green atop blue shorts. GOAT uni.

Like MJ in a 23. Red, black, and white.

Again it’s the smell.

And it’s learning that the smell is from the glue.

And learning that some of my favorite companies were started by Nazis.

It’s soccer being the only thing that matters.

It’s my mom explaining why it’s a beautiful game: all you need is two spatial points and an object that moves on the ground.

Two rocks. A rock and a tree. A fence and your sweatshirt. And something that rolls when you kick it.

You don’t need gear. You need heart.

You need creativity. It’s art. You make magic with two feet and a ball.

It’s about being the best player — by far — in a rec league when we moved here.

So good and such a chutzpahn that I asked the coach to let me play goalie so I could dribble the ball across the field, meg the other keeper, get on the ground, and head the ball across the line.

Showoff.

It’s about being at summer camp and scoring the best goal of my life. Right upper ninety from way out.

Rami the music guy thought I’d be the next Ronaldo.

It’s placing second in the Haifa city-wide indoor soccer tournament.

It’s playing center mid.

It’s winning silver in the Bergen County JV tourney. Stunning preppy kids from Ridgewood and Pascack Valley.

Singing “We Are the Champions” in a yellow school bus on the ride home from the semis and then being completely and utterly humiliated by Ramapo in the final.

It’s realizing I won’t be the best. I don’t have the speed. Or the stamina. Or the talent.

That I can’t really compete.

It’s the larger-than-life assistant coach who didn’t know the game but had a sense of humor and was a bully and seemed so happy.

And then learning that he jumped in front of a train.

Again, it’s what it means to wear something that looks like a check versus three simple stripes.

It’s associating those stripes with peace and later wondering if the brand was originally inspired by soldiers earning shoulder marks.

It’s about a pacifist mom not wanting me to be in the military.

About moving to America at seven.

And later as an adolescent shooting three blanks from an M16.

The power. The terror. The possibility of destruction from a weapon like that.

It’s always wanting to create.

It’s losing the love of the game. And sports. And competition.

It’s skipping SportsCenter in the morning.

It’s finding a new love: music.

It’s the song you write that makes you cry. And realizing it doesn’t make anybody else cry. And recording it anyway.

It’s about making something, anything.

It’s wearing the swoosh for a while and feeling like I’m fronting. A poser. A fraud.

It’s about coming back to the brand I loved.

It’s about walking into Dick’s Sporting Goods on Route 22 in my mid-thirties and feeling like I’m seven again.

And it’s the smell of gifted cologne I told Dana I didn’t like. And then smelling it on someone else and realizing she was 100% right.

It’s the smell of an unused studio. Of well worn headphones. Of an old guitar.

It’s the smell of these new size 3K Sambas for my eleven-month-old Margot.

It’s the Copa Mundials I’ll get her.

Or even better, it’s the pair that speak to her.

It’s the smell of new shoes.

It’s the smell of a smiley, laughing baby.

It’s the smell of opportunity.

It’s the smell of the future will be better.

It’s the smell.

It’s love.

South Orange, New Jersey
2026

Words, photographs & narration by Ron Seidel
